materials.content: data:image/jpeg;base64,

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
Address Allocation

Subnetting at its core is a mathematical exercise driven by binary logic. To confidently design,

troubleshoot, or maintain a network, an administrator must master the underlying mathematics

of IP address allocation. This module breaks down the conversion mechanics, fundamental

algebraic formulas, and structural rules used to split an IP space into distinct segments.

subtopics.title: Subtopic 1: The Binary Foundation: Network vs. Host Bits

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
Every IPv4 address is a 32-bit binary number disguised as four decimal octets (e.g.,

192.168.1.50). A subnet mask determines exactly how many of those 32 bits belong to the

network and how many belong to local hosts.

When you subnet a network, you perform a process called bit borrowing. You take bits that

originally belonged to the host portion and reassign them to the network portion.

● 1s in a subnet mask represent the immutable network address.

● 0s in a subnet mask represent the flexible host addresses.

Borrowing a bit splits an existing block exactly in half. Because binary is a base-2 system, every

single bit you borrow exponentially multiplies the number of available networks while

simultaneously halving the available host space.

The Core Mathematical Formulas

To calculate exactly what happens when you modify a network mask, you must utilize two

essential formulas.

Formula A: Calculating Subnets ($2^n$)

To find out how many unique subnets you can create by borrowing bits, use the expression:

$$\text{Total Subnets} = 2^n$$

Where $n$ is the number of bits borrowed from the host portion of the default classful mask.

● Example: If you take a default Class C network (/24) and borrow 3 bits to make it a /27,

you calculate $2^3 = 8$. You have successfully carved your single network into 8

separate subnets.

Formula B: Calculating Usable Hosts ($2^h - 2$)

To determine how many physical devices (computers, printers, router interfaces) can live inside

each of those new subnets, use the expression:

$$\text{Usable Hosts} = 2^h - 2$$

Where $h$ is the number of bits remaining in the host portion (out of the original 32 total bits).

subtopics.title: Subtopic 2: The Structural Rule: Why Subtract Two?

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
The $-2$ in the host formula is a non-negotiable rule in IPv4 engineering. In every single subnet

block generated, two addresses are automatically rendered unusable for assignment to physical

endpoints:

1. The Network ID (First Address): This occurs when all host bits are set to binary 0. It

represents the identity of the entire sub-network itself. Routers look at this address to

forward data across the internet.

2. The Broadcast ID (Last Address): This occurs when all host bits are set to binary 1. If

a device wants to transmit a packet to every single device within its local subnet

simultaneously, it targets this specific destination address.

Because these two operational boundaries must exist, a subnet block with a total size of 64

addresses can only ever accommodate 62 actual physical devices.

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
Let's look at an explicit example. Suppose you start with the network 192.168.1.0/24 and need

to split it by borrowing 2 bits.

● New Prefix length: $24 + 2 = \mathbf{/26}$

● Subnets Created: $2^2 = \mathbf{4\text{ subnets}}$

● Remaining Host Bits: $32 - 26 = \mathbf{6\text{ host bits}}$

● Total Block Size per Subnet: $2^6 = \mathbf{64\text{ addresses}}$

● Usable Hosts per Subnet: $2^6 - 2 = \mathbf{62\text{ hosts}}$

The mathematical "magic number" or block size is 64. This tells you exactly how to increment

your network boundaries:

Subnet

#

Network ID 	First Usable IP Last Usable IP Broadcast ID

Subnet

1

192.168.1.0 	192.168.1.1 	192.168.1.62 	192.168.1.63

Subnet

2

192.168.1.64 	192.168.1.65 	192.168.1.126 	192.168.1.127

Subnet

3

192.168.1.128 	192.168.1.129 	192.168.1.190 	192.168.1.191

Subnet

4

192.168.1.192 	192.168.1.193 	192.168.1.254 	192.168.1.255

materials.title: Subnetting was hard until I learned this shortcut

topics.title: Topic 2: The Shift from FLSM to VLSM

materials.title: Reference Image
materials.content: data:image/jpeg;base64,

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
(Variable Length Subnet Masking)

While basic subnetting relies on dividing a network into equal, mathematically uniform chunks,

real-world networks are rarely uniform. A corporate headquarters might have 500 employees, a

branch office might have 50, and the secure server room might only need 10 IP addresses. This

module explores how network engineers moved away from rigid, wasteful designs to a flexible,

highly optimized approach.

subtopics.title: Subtopic 1: The Problem with Fixed Length Subnet Masking (FLSM)

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
In the early days of IP addressing, engineers used FLSM (Fixed Length Subnet Masking).

Under FLSM, once you chose a subnet mask, you had to apply it to every subnet across your

entire network. It was a "one-size-fits-all" approach.

The Flaw of FLSM:

Imagine you have the network 192.168.1.0/24 and you need to create subnets for four different

departments. You borrow 2 bits to create 4 subnets, resulting in a /26 mask for everything.

● As calculated in Module 1, a /26 provides 62 usable hosts per subnet.

● This is perfect if every department has roughly 50 to 60 devices.

● But what about the WAN links (Wide Area Network connections)? A connection

between two distinct routers only requires exactly 2 IP addresses (one for each end of

the cable).

● Under FLSM, you are forced to assign a /26 block to that router link. You use 2

addresses, and the remaining 60 addresses sit permanently empty and unusable. This is

a massive waste of valuable IP space.

2. Enter VLSM: Subnetting a Subnet

To stop this waste, VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking) was introduced. VLSM allows

network administrators to use different subnet masks for different subnets within the same major

network block.

Instead of slicing the pie into equal pieces, VLSM allows you to slice the pie into a massive

piece for the Sales team, a medium piece for IT, and tiny, precise slivers for your router

connections. You achieve this by essentially taking one of your subnets and subnetting it again.

subtopics.title: Subtopic 2: The Golden Rule of VLSM: Largest to Smallest

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
Designing a VLSM network requires a very specific operational order. To avoid overlapping IP

ranges and mathematical chaos, you must always fulfill the requirement for the largest

number of hosts first, and work your way down to the smallest.

The Step-by-Step Process:

1. List all requirements: Write down every department/segment and how many hosts they

need.

2. Sort descending: Arrange them from the largest host requirement to the smallest.

3. Calculate the closest block: For the largest group, use the $2^h - 2$ formula to find the

smallest block size that will fit them.

4. Assign and increment: Assign the network ID, calculate the broadcast ID, and then

start the next department's network at the very next available IP address.

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
Let's take our standard Class C network, 192.168.1.0/24 (256 total addresses), and apply VLSM

for a company with the following needs:

● Headquarters: Needs 100 hosts

● Branch Office: Needs 50 hosts

● Router-to-Router WAN Link: Needs 2 hosts

Step 1: The Headquarters (100 Hosts)

● We need a block that holds at least 100. $2^7 = 128$. (126 usable hosts).

● This requires 7 host bits. $32 - 7 = 25$. Our mask is /25.

● Range: 192.168.1.0 to 192.168.1.127.

Step 2: The Branch Office (50 Hosts)

● The next available IP is 192.168.1.128. We start here.

● We need a block for 50. $2^6 = 64$. (62 usable hosts).

● This requires 6 host bits. $32 - 6 = 26$. Our mask is /26.

● Range: 192.168.1.128 to 192.168.1.191.

Step 3: The WAN Link (2 Hosts)

● The next available IP is 192.168.1.192.

● We need a block for exactly 2. $2^2 = 4$. (2 usable hosts).

● This requires 2 host bits. $32 - 2 = 30$. Our mask is /30.

● Range: 192.168.1.192 to 192.168.1.195.

By using VLSM, we successfully accommodated all networks, perfectly tailored the subnet

masks (/25, /26, and /30), and still have unused IP space left over from 192.168.1.196 to 255 for

future growth!

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
Using Binary ANDing

While humans comfortably read IP addresses in decimal format (like 192.168.1.50), networking

hardware such as routers and switches can only think in binary (1s and 0s). When a packet

arrives at a router, the device must instantly determine whether the destination host sits on the

immediate local network or if the packet needs to be forwarded to an external gateway.

To accomplish this at lightning speed, hardware uses a fundamental logical process known as

Bitwise ANDing. This module breaks down the logic gates, binary conversions, and routing

table lookups that happen under the hood every time data moves across a network.

subtopics.title: Subtopic 1: The Logic of the AND Gate

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
In computer science, an AND operation is a Boolean logic gate that compares two inputs and

produces a single output. The mathematical rule for an AND gate is simple: the output is only

a 1 if BOTH inputs are 1. If either input (or both) is a 0, the output is automatically 0.

$$\begin{aligned} 1 \text{ AND } 1 &= 1 \\ 1 \text{ AND } 0 &= 0 \\ 0 \text{ AND } 1 &= 0 \\ 0 \text{

AND } 0 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

A router utilizes this exact logic to mask out the host portion of an IP address, leaving behind

only the clean Network ID.

The Step-by-Step ANDing Process

When a packet is processed, the router aligns the 32-bit binary destination IP address directly

above the 32-bit binary subnet mask. It then performs an AND operation on every single bit

position from left to right.

Let's look at how a router processes a destination IP of 192.168.1.150 with a subnet mask of

255.255.255.192 (/26).

Step A: Convert to Binary

First, the decimal numbers are converted into their 8-bit binary octet equivalents:

● IP Address (192.168.1.150): 11000000.10101000.00000001.10010110

● Subnet Mask (255.255.255.192): 11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000

Step B: Perform the Bitwise AND

Next, the router stacks them and compares each column:

Plaintext

IP Address: 11000000 . 10101000 . 00000001 . 10010110

Subnet Mask: 11111111 . 11111111 . 11111111 . 11000000

------------------------------------------------------

Result: 11000000 . 10101000 . 00000001 . 10000000

Notice what happened in the final octet:

● The IP address had 10010110 (150).

● The mask had 11000000 (192).

● When ANDed together, only the first bit remained a 1 because that was the only position

where both lines had a 1. The remaining bits were completely wiped out to 0.

Step C: Convert Back to Decimal

The router takes the final binary result (11000000.10101000.00000001.10000000) and

translates it back into a standard decimal notation: 192.168.1.128.

This calculated number is the official Network ID. The router now knows exactly which

sub-network this packet belongs to.

subtopics.title: Subtopic 2: How the Router Uses the Result

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
Once the Network ID is calculated, the router looks at its internal Routing Table. A routing table

is essentially a directory that maps Network IDs to specific physical exit ports (interfaces) on the

router.

● If the calculated Network ID matches a network directly attached to one of the router's

local ports, the router encapsulates the data and sends it straight to the local switch.

● If the Network ID matches a remote network somewhere else on the internet, the table

tells the router which neighboring router to pass it to next (the "Next Hop").

● If the Network ID doesn't match anything in the table, the router sends it to its "Gateway

of Last Resort" (Default Gateway) or drops the packet entirely.

Because ANDing is a hardware-level mathematical calculation, modern routers can perform this

operation millions of times per second, ensuring minimal latency across network paths.

topics.title: Topic 4: Subnetting in the IPv6 Era

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
For decades, the entire science of IPv4 subnetting has been driven by a mindset of absolute

scarcity. Because the IPv4 protocol is limited to roughly 4.3 billion total addresses, network

engineers have been forced into complex mathematical gymnastics (like VLSM) to avoid

wasting a single usable IP. IPv6 completely flips this paradigm. This module explores how the

massive scale of 128-bit addressing transforms subnetting from a frantic conservation effort into

a clean, hierarchical organizational strategy.

subtopics.title: Subtopic 1: The End of the Address Shortage

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
The most defining characteristic of IPv6 is its sheer size. While IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses, IPv6

uses 128-bit addresses, written in hexadecimal format (e.g.,

2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334).

To put this into perspective, 128 bits allow for $3.4 \times 10^{38}$ unique addresses. That is

enough to assign an IP address to every single atom on the surface of the Earth. Because of

this unimaginably massive pool, engineers no longer need to worry about conserving host

addresses. The question is no longer "How many hosts can fit in this subnet?" but rather "How

do we logically group our global network traffic?"

The Anatomy of an IPv6 Address and the /64 Standard

In IPv6, the standard practice is to draw a permanent line directly down the middle of the

address. The first 64 bits are dedicated entirely to routing the packet to the correct network, and

the last 64 bits are dedicated solely to identifying the specific device (called the Interface ID).

Therefore, the standard subnet size for every single local network in the world is a /64.

● Inside a single /64 subnet, there are $2^{64}$ host addresses (over 18 quintillion).

● Whether it is a point-to-point router link, a small home network, or an entire stadium's

Wi-Fi, you assign it a /64.

● Why? This standardization allows for SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration).

SLAAC is a feature where a device (like your smartphone) can plug into a network,

automatically grab the 64-bit network prefix from the local router, and generate its own

unique 64-bit Interface ID without needing a DHCP server.

subtopics.title: Subtopic 2: How IPv6 Subnetting Actually Works

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
If every local network gets a /64, how do you actually subnet? You do it by utilizing the bits

before the 64-bit boundary.

Instead of borrowing bits from the host space (as in IPv4), an organization is assigned a large

block of network prefixes from their Internet Service Provider (ISP), and they use the remaining

bits up to the 64-bit line to organize their internal structure.

The Three Parts of an IPv6 Subnet:

1. Global Routing Prefix: This is the block assigned to the company by the ISP (usually a

/48). It identifies the company to the rest of the global internet.

2. Subnet ID: This is the space between the ISP prefix and the /64 boundary. The

company uses these bits to create their internal subnets.

3. Interface ID: The last 64 bits, identifying the physical host.

subtopics.title: Subtopic 3: A Practical IPv6 Design Example

materials.title: Lecture Reading
materials.content:
Imagine your company is assigned the prefix 2001:db8:acad::/48 by your ISP.

Because the standard host boundary is at /64, you have 16 bits in the middle dedicated entirely

to your Subnet ID ($64 - 48 = 16$).

Using the formula $2^{16}$, you can create 65,536 unique subnets for your company. Because

IPv6 is written in hexadecimal, counting and assigning these subnets is incredibly clean and

easy to read. You simply increment the 4th hexadecimal block:

● Subnet 1 (Sales): 2001:db8:acad:0001::/64

● Subnet 2 (HR): 2001:db8:acad:0002::/64

● Subnet 3 (IT): 2001:db8:acad:0003::/64

● ...and so on, all the way up to ffff (65,535).

There is no complex binary ANDing required to figure out where your network boundaries end.

The hierarchy is baked right into the visual structure of the address, resulting in routing tables

that are highly optimized and incredibly easy to troubleshoot.
